coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of self-targeted carbon nanodot for efficient cancer cell imaging and therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Noorkhajavi, Ghasem
Abdian, Nesa
Najaflou, Meysam
Hefferon, Kathleen
Yari-Khosroushahi, Ahmad
Shahgolzari, Mehdi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) possess a broad spectrum of applications in chemical, physical, and biomedical research. We are particularly intrigued by the utilization of CDs as fluorescence nanoparticles for the purpose of targeted imaging of tumor cells. Here, we have achieved the one-step synthesis of luminous CDs utilizing pemetrexed (PMX) as the carbon precursor in aqueous environment via the hydrothermal technique. The average size of CDs was about 3.4 nm. The CDs were internalized within 4T1and MCF-7 cell lines. The CDs wasn’t exhibited toxicity in 4T1 cell line. However, an increase of co-localization of CDs, and the cytotoxic effects in high concentrations was observed in MCF-7 cells, which probably can result from the interaction of these nanoparticles with the folate receptor (FR). The presence of PMX residuals, analog with FA in CDs resulted in a remarkable ability to specifically target cancer cells and effectively enhance the cellular uptake mediated by FR. This achievement demonstrates significant potential for application in biological and bioimaging research endeavors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Pemetrexed
Cellular imaging
Therapeutic agents
Folate receptors

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research protocol was approved and Supported by the Student Research Committee, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences (grant number: 71619).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the CDs synthesis method.

Characterization of CDs. (A) Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis of CDs. (B) zeta potential. (C) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs, (D) scanning electron microscopic (SEM) of CDs displaying ultra-small spherical nanoparticles.

Optical spectra of the fluorescent CDs. (A) UV–vis absorption (Abs), (B) Fluorescence emission spectra (in various wavelengths) of the CDs.(C) Photostability under UV exposure for CDs (D) FTIR spectra of Pemetrexed, and carbon dots.

(A, B, C) XRD diffraction pattern of CDs. (D) EDS spectra for CDs. (E) Elemental mapping images of CDs for the presence and distribution of C, O, N, Cu, and S elements, which are spread in almost all parts.

Comparison of fluorescence images for different cell samples in the presence of CDs (1 mg/ml) (5 cells from each image were selected for quantizing fluorescence intensity). The (A) 4T1, (B) MCF7 cells incubated for 1 h and 4 h with CDs. (C) MCF-7 and were pretreated at 37 °C for 2 h with excess folic acid for folate receptor saturation and then incubated with CDs (1 mg/ml) at 37 °C for 4 h. (D) Comparison of fluorescence intensity for 4T1 and MCF-7 in the presence of CDs.

MTT assay and flow cytometry. (A) 4T1 and MCF74T1 cells viability in the CD concentrations (o, 200, 400, 600, 800 and 1000 µg/mL) for 24 h. The apoptotic effects of cells, determined by flow cytometry after 24 h for control and 850 µg/ml of CDs for (B) 4T1 cells (C) MCF-7 cells.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of self-targeted carbon nanodot for efficient cancer cell imaging and therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Student Research Committee, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Student Research Committee, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Virology Laboratory, Department of Cell & Systems Biology, University of Toronto, Toronto, ON M5S 3B2, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Virology Laboratory, Department of Cell & Systems Biology, University of Toronto, Toronto, ON M5S 3B2, Canada

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) possess a broad spectrum of applications in chemical, physical, and biomedical research. We are particularly intrigued by the utilization of CDs as fluorescence nanoparticles for the purpose of targeted imaging of tumor cells. Here, we have achieved the one-step synthesis of luminous CDs utilizing pemetrexed (PMX) as the carbon precursor in aqueous environment via the hydrothermal technique. The average size of CDs was about 3.4 nm. The CDs were internalized within 4T1and MCF-7 cell lines. The CDs wasn’t exhibited toxicity in 4T1 cell line. However, an increase of co-localization of CDs, and the cytotoxic effects in high concentrations was observed in MCF-7 cells, which probably can result from the interaction of these nanoparticles with the folate receptor (FR). The presence of PMX residuals, analog with FA in CDs resulted in a remarkable ability to specifically target cancer cells and effectively enhance the cellular uptake mediated by FR. This achievement demonstrates significant potential for application in biological and bioimaging research endeavors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence imaging of cancer cells is a versatile method in cancer research [1]. Fluorescence imaging visually depicts the manifestation and function of molecules, cells, and biological processes that influence tumor behavior and drug reactions [2]. Conventional fluorescent agents (e.g. dyes, fluorescent proteins) are widely used for cell labelling, but have limitations in their chemical complexity and cell targetability [3]. Fluorescent nanoparticles like quantum dots (QDs), polymer dots (PDs), nanodiamonds (NDs), and upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) are gaining attention as bright, photostable probes to address traditional probe shortcomings [2,4,5]. Fluorescent nanomaterials, with their enhanced fluorescent brightness, photostability, water dispersibility, and biocompatibility, hold significant potential for cancer diagnosis and treatment [6]. The enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect of nanomaterials can provide precise tumor treatment with fewer potential side effects [7]. However, specific cell targeting of fluorescent nanoparticles is crucial for improving patient management by optimizing detection efficiency and treatment efficacy [8,9]. Fluorescence imaging targets tumor cells by coupling a fluorescent agent with biological probes that recognize specific tumor markers, labelled using fluorescent markers or complex molecular assemblies [1].

Fluorescent nanoparticles are commonly conjugated with targeting ligands like transferrin, folic acid (FA), and peptides to bind to the corresponding biomarker [9]. FA, a low-molecular weight vitamin, is crucial for cell survival and has a high affinity for binding with the folate receptor (FR), a cancer biomarker [2,10]. FR levels are significantly higher in epithelial malignant tumors, particularly in ovarian, endometrial, kidney, and breast cancers, and positively correlate with tumor grade and stage [11]. FA, folate analogues, or folate complex can enter cancer cells through receptor-mediated endocytosis by binding to cancer cell FR [7]. FR expression levels can differentiate between cancer and normal cells, making FA a promising ligand for cancer detection and diagnosis due to its ability to differentiate cancer cells [2]. Researchers are exploring the use of FA functionalization on nanomaterials to enable targeted fluorescence imaging of cancer cells, a widely researched application [2,12]. However, used materials are costly, require complex preparation methods, and can have potentially hazardous effects. Multi-functionalization steps during covalent conjugation of FA onto fluorescent nanomaterials may negatively impact their fluorescence intensity and suspension stability of the particulate system [3]. The urgent need for cost-effective, easily fabricated, and non-cytotoxic nanomaterials for targeted fluorescence imaging of cancer cells is urgent.

Carbon dots (CDs) are quasi-spherical nanoscale particles (<10 nm) with a carbon building block, providing metal-free photoluminescence (PL) technology [13]. CDs are non-toxic, soluble in water, chemically stable, broad excitation/emission spectral, low photobleaching, and biocompatibility, with easy surface functionalization due to surface sp3 carbon atoms. These devices are utilized in various applications such as bioimaging, drug delivery, catalysis, optoelectronic devices, white light-emitting diodes, and biomedical theranostics, integrating diagnostics with therapeutics [14–17]. CDs produce from various carbon sources, such as small organic molecules (e.g. citrate, folic acid), plants tissues (e.g. leaves, flowers, fruits, and peels) [18–22]. CDs synthesis can be achieved using top-down or bottom-up methods. Top-down methods face challenges like expensive equipment and laborious processes, leading to high production costs. Bottom-up methods like microwave, hydrothermal, and wet-chemical offer simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and environmental friendliness. The hydrothermal method is a straightforward and scalable technique that promotes high graphitization, allowing direct interaction with the target, expanding potential applications [19,21,23,24]. Recent research has focused on doping and targeting CDs to enhance their properties. Heteroatom doping in CDs has shown excellent optical properties, including fluorescence, with high quantum yield.[16,17,21]. Furthermore, CDs with polar properties often require active mechanisms for cellular uptake. [16]. For example, Ma et al. developed fluorescent CDs covalently conjugated with FA for targeted fluorescence imaging of human cervical carcinoma cells, marking a new avenue in cancer research [25]. Ouyang et al. developed a fluorescence probe using FA to functionalize fluorescent CDs for cancer cell imaging in various cell mixtures, with minimal fluorescence background [26]. Zhang and colleagues enhanced blue fluorescent CDs with FA for effective imaging targeting of HeLa and HepG-2 cancer cells [27]. Kushwaha et al. demonstrated the dual targeting capability of a fluorescence nanoprobe using covalently conjugated fluorescent CDs, showing excellent cytoplasm and nucleus targeting performance with MCF-7 breast cancer cells [28]. The current quantum yields of folate receptor-targeted fluorescent CDs are less than 10 %, making the development of targeted fluorescence CDs with easy preparation and good luminescence a challenge. It is shown that carbon dots obtained from FA and that the in cellule observed photoluminescence results from the interaction of these nanoparticles with the folic acid-receptor [29].

Here, we synthesized CDs from a drug that residues of FA in CDs can promote CDs uptake. Fluorescent CDs with residues of FA were first produced through a one-step hydrothermal process by Pemetrexed (PMX) as a carbon source. PMX is a chemotherapeutic FA analogue and is used in malignant pleural mesothelioma (MPM), pancreatic, lung, gastric, and breast cancer treatment [30]. PMX interferes with DNA and RNA biosynthesis. Similar to other drugs, PMX has side effects like alopecia, bradycardia, and nausea which makes its medical applications limited [31]. PMT-derived CDs selectively stained cancer cells expressing folate receptors, confirming a direct relationship between cell labelling and folate receptor abundance. The newly designed CDs can aid in identifying agonists/antagonists for the folate receptor, aiding in the development of new cancer therapeutics.

Pemetrexed (PMX, 100 mg vial) was purchased from Neshat Darou Saveh Pharmaceutical Co, dialysis tubing (benzoylated, MWCO ∼ 1 kDa), RPMI-1640 folate free media, and methylthiazolyldiphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich.

Fluorescent CDs were synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal protocol carbonization using PMX carbon source. PMX (20 mg) was dissolved in 20 ml of deionized water. The clear light yellow solution was transferred into a reactor and then heated at 270 °C for 5 h. The pyrolysis temperature is considered to be a significant element that has the potential to impact the optical characteristics of PMT-CDs. Previous studies have revealed that in order to achieve a CD with a high Quantum yield (QY), the source material must undergo a heating process that exceeds its melting point [32]. It is shown that raising the reaction temperature can result in a reduction in the average size of the nanodots from 20.35 to 6.48 nm, accompanied by an increase in their quantum yield from 13 % to 18 % [33]. A clear yellow–brown monodisperse solution was gained due to carbonization. The solution was dialyzed against water by using a dialysis membrane for purification of resulting carbon dots and removal of unreacted reagents. CDs powder was acquired through lyophilizing (Fig. 1 ). Photostability measurements were conducted employing a UV lamp that emitted an excitation light of 365 nm wavelength. The samples, which consisted of aqueous solutions containing solely carbon dots, were positioned in front of the UV light within a dimly lit chamber for a duration spanning several hours. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensities were recorded at various time intervals [34].

The QYs of the CDs (in water) were determined by plotting the integrated photoluminescence (PL) intensity against the absorbance value. The QY of CDs was estimated using the equation Q = Qs (Fs/Fx)(Ax/As)(ns/nx)2 [17,35]. Where Q is the quantum yield, F is the integral of the fluorescence emission scan, A is the absorbance, η is the index of refraction of the solvent, x indicates the type of sample to be analyzed, and s denotes the reference material. Quinine sulfate (with a fluorescence quantum yield of 0.54) was selected as the standard fluorescent agent.

The particle size of synthesized CDs was determined using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, ZEISS EM900) and dynamic light scattering (DLS, Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern Instruments, and Malvern, UK). The morphology of particles was examined using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Tescan MIRA3, and Czech Republic). The elemental composition and mapping analysis of particles were examined by energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS, Oxford, United Kingdom). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the particles was used to investigate the crystallinity of carbon dots via an X-ray diffractometer (Tongda, China) with Cu-Kα radiation. The data was collected in the 2θ range from 10° to 50° with a step size of 0.02° and step time of 0.5 s. The functional groups of the synthesized particles were identified using fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR, Bruker Tesnsor 27, Germany) in the range of 500–4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 1 cm−1.

Tumor cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium, incubated, and then treated with CDs. The cells were washed three times with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) to remove unassociated compounds. Fluorescence imaging was performed on the cells, and competition experiments were conducted to confirm receptor-mediated uptake. In this regard, MCF-7 cells were pre-treated with 50 μL of FA solution for 2 h before CD treatment. The cells were then used for fluorescence imaging. The analysis of the image was done using ImageJ software and fluorescence intensity was calculated using the corrected total cell fluorescence (CTCF) value. [CTCF = Integrated Density–(Area of selected cell X Mean fluorescence of background readings)].

The cytotoxicity of CDs was assessed on 4T1 (cells which do not express detectable levels of FR) and MCF-7 cells (cells which express detectable levels of FR) using a standard MTT assay. The cells were seeded in 96-well plates and then added to varying concentrations of CDs (0, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000 μg/ml). The cells were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, then MTT solution was added. The formazan was liberated and the cell viability rate was calculated.

The death cells underwent examination using the FACS flow cytometer. Cells were placed in six-well plates along with CDs (850 μg/ml) and incubated at a temperature of 37 °C in a CO2 incubator for a duration of 12 h. Subsequently, each well was exposed to a laser with a wavelength of 808 nm. The cells were subjected to trypsinization, centrifugation, and washing with cold PBS. The resulting cell pellets were then suspended in 100 μL of annexin binding buffer and incubated under the prescribed culture conditions for a period of 15 min. Following this, 5 μL of annexin V and 5 μL of PI were introduced and incubated at room temperature for 15 min. The FACSC flow cytometer (Becton Dickinson) was utilized to investigate the percentage of early and late apoptosis.

All experiments were performed in triplicate, and the outcomes of one representative data set were reported. The data were presented as the mean SD. Statistical differences in the studied parameters between the samples were analyzed by using ANOVA with the Tukey-Kramer post-test using Excel and Prism 9 software (GraphPad Software Inc.). P values less than 0.05 were considered significant.

When the light yellow PMX solution was exposed to microwave heating, it underwent a transformation in color. It turned from colorless to a clear yellow–brown monodisperse solution after dialysis of the solution against ultrapure water and emitted blue fluorescence when exposed to UV light with a wavelength of 365 nm. This alteration in the solution's colour, coupled with the appearance of blue fluorescence, suggests the creation of CDs. According to various theories, the initiation of fluorescence in CDs is attributed to structural anomalies within them, primarily arising from the presence of sp2 and sp3 carbon clusters in specific proportions [36]. Furthermore, the residues of functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxylic) on the surface of CDs also serve as a critical factor in eliminating energy traps and causing fluorescence when exposed to UV light [36,37].

The DLS data reveals that the CDs exhibit monodispersity with hydrodynamic particle size about 4.08 nm (Fig. 2 A) and zeta potential −4.42 mV (Fig. 2B). The negative surface charge of particles means they not only have high stability in circulation and are less likely to interact with environmental factors but also have less toxicity to cells [38]. Furthermore, the size and morphology of the CDs were determined by TEM (Fig. 2C) and SEM (Fig. 2D). TEM observation clearly confirmed that the CDs were successfully obtained; they displayed a spherical morphology, good dispersity without apparent aggregation, and an average size of 3.5 nm. The results of TEM images correspond to the DLS measurement. The size of the synthesized CDs measured through TEM imaging was smaller in comparison to the one measured via DLS. The liquid phases in the DLS method is the main reason for this phenomenon [39]. Also, during the preparation process in TEM, dehydration occurs and causes the shrinkage of particles [40].

The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of the CDs were investigated. The UV–vis spectrum depicts a maximum absorption peak at 276 nm, which was attributed to the n–p* and p–p* transition of CO and the p–p* transition of CC (Fig. 3 A). To further investigate their optical properties, the fluorescence spectra of the CDs were recorded at gradually increasing excitation wavelengths (Fig. 3B). A gradual red shift, altered fluorescence intensity with increasing excitation wavelength, and two emission peaks (less than 400 nm) can be observed; this can be explained by the “edge red-shift effect” and a dependence on the excitation wavelength. The quantum yield of CD nanoparticles was determined to be 20.1 % based on the study conducted by Williams et al. [17,35],which investigated the excitation and emission properties of these nanoparticles. The photostability of luminescent nanomaterials is crucial for assessing their applications, especially in C-dots as potential fluorescent probes for cell and tissue imaging, considering fluorescence emission intensity during continuous excitation [34]. The fluorescence intensity of CDs maintained 65 % of their initial emission intensity after 6 h of UV light exposure (Fig. 3C). This highlights the importance of photostability in evaluating luminescent nanomaterials' applications.

FTIR analysis demonstrated a similar observation, revealing the existence of various carbon-bonded, nitrogen-bonded, and oxygen-bonded components resulting from the synthesis process utilizing PMX as the reagent for the formation of CDs (Fig. 3D). It revealed bands at 3284 cm−1, 2935 cm−1, 1640 cm−1, 1593 cm−1, and 1405 cm−1 related to the O-H and primary amin (N-H), C-H stretching, –COOH, CC aromatic, O-H bending respectively. Also, the C-H bending and drug carboxyl groups were detected at around 1087 cm−1 and 512 cm−1, respectively. The main wavelengths of PMX were also detected in CDs FT spectra at 3400 cm−1 assigned to O-H and N-H. Other peaks include C-H aliphatic at 2921 cm−1, and 2853 cm−1, CN at 1624 cm−1 [41–43].

Fig. 4 A-C demonstrates the XRD analysis of CDs. There is a large number of peaks at 2θ values of 20-30° and between 30 and 40° on the XRD of CDs which proves it has an accurately stable crystalline structure (Fig. 4A). The dominant amorphous character of the CDs is also reflected in the broad hump centered on ∼ 2θ = 25-26° in the XRD profile shown corresponding to a lattice spacing of 0.34 nm (Fig. 4B-C). Such a hump has been widely observed in XRD patterns of amorphous carbon (45 nature). Also, the obtained XRD pattern approximately matched with the standard diffraction pattern [Ref. code 00–030-1926] [44]. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) with elemental mapping was used to determine the surface elemental analysis of CDs. It shows that the surface of the CDs mainly consists of the O, C, S, N, and Na at the same time (Fig. 4D-E). The existence of dynamic constituents like oxygen establishes the fundamental framework for the process of functionalizing the surfaces of CDs, thereby mitigating toxicity and safeguarding their overall integrity and stability [17].

Folate receptor-based ligands such as FA and methotrexate are used in research to synthesize CDs with high fluorescence quantum yields for imaging, and targetability towards positive folate tumor cells [45,46]. These studies show that the residues of FA in CDs can promote folate receptor-mediated cellular uptake. It is demonstrated, the direct interaction and the selectivity of this interaction between carbon dots obtained from folic acid and the soluble form of the folic acid receptor [29]. In this regard, we are specifically interested in studying new analogue-based FA, PMX-derived CDs. The cellular uptake of CDs into human mammary carcinoma (MCF-7, cells which express detectable levels of FR) (Fig. 5 A) and 4T1 cells (cells which do not express detectable levels of FR) (Fig. 5B) was assessed through fluorescence microscopy. MCF-7 and 4T1 cells were treated with high concentrations of CDs (850 μg/ml) in 1 and 4 h. This fluorescence accumulation suggests the internalization of CDs by both MCF-7 and 4T1 cells. Quantitative uptake analysis with image-j software showed that 4T1cells showed a lower fluorescence intensity than MCF-7 cells after incubation periods of 1 h and 4 h. MCF-7 cells show significant enhancement in intracellular fluorescence in a short time, after incubation time of 1 h. When being incubated with CDs for 4 h, 4T1 cells still show much lower fluorescence intensity than MCF-7. It can be because of the folate residues of the CDs and the overexpressing of FR on the surface of MCF-7. Pre-incubation of cells with excess free folic acid led to a reduction in the cellular fluorescence intensity of CDs (Fig. 5C). This reduction was more prominent in MCF-7 cells, which overexpress folate receptors, compared to 4T1 cells with lower folate receptor expression. This observation suggests that folate receptors play a role in the cellular uptake of CDs, particularly in folate receptor-rich MCF-7 cells. The time-dependent increase in fluorescence accumulation indicates the progressive uptake of CDs over the incubation period. Moreover, the competitive uptake inhibition study implicates the involvement of folate receptors in the cellular uptake process, particularly in FR-overexpressing MCF-7 cells (Fig. 5D). These findings collectively highlight the potential of CDs as targeted delivery vehicles for cancer cells that exhibit folate receptor-mediated uptake mechanisms. Several publications demonstrate, the capacity of FA, MTX-derived CDs to target FR in a cellular medium [3,45,46]. Recently, synthesized CDs derived from FA, through the utilization of a folate-binding protein, a soluble variant of FR with a notable affinity FA, its inherent ligand, established the development of a complex between the two entities and the specificity of this interaction [29]. It demonstrated that a much lower CDs concentration of around 0.2 mg/ml is required for the cell imaging experiments [3]. Recently, FA-CDs can accurately identify FR expression on cancer cell membranes and positive FR cells in cell mixes of MCF-7/HepG-2 and HepG-2/PC-12 cells. These findings suggest FA-CDs could be useful for accurately separating cancer cells from normal ones [2].

PMX has been reported to have anticancer properties due to its high affinity binding to the folate receptor and similar transport kinetics to methotrexate [47]. It targets different enzymes from the folate cycle, mainly dihydrofolate reductase and thymidylate synthase, which have been used to cure many patients [48]. Inspired by chemotherapeutic FA analog properties of PMX, we hypothesize that PMX can be used to synthesize CDs that retain their therapeutic. Therefore, cytotoxic of CDs with self-targeting theranostic ability were assayed via MTT and FACSC. MTT assay showed no cytotoxicity for 4T1 and MCF-7 cell lines at lower concentrations in 24 h (Fig. 6 A). Cell viability exceeding 100 % is unusual in 4T1 cells competing with MCF-7 cells, and could be due to stimulatory effects, such as nutrient presence or cellular signaling pathways. Negatively charged CDs may shield cytotoxicity by preventing strong interactions with 4T1 cells, allowing 4T1 cells to maintain high viability. But the shielding effect can reduce in MCF-7 due to the high affinity between CDs and FR. Kong et al. showed that high cell viability of DNA aptamer AS1411 modified CDs with polyethyleneimine (PEI) can be due to the nanoparticle's negative charge MCF-7[49]. The viability of HeLa cells was found to be over 95 % even at a high concentration of 500 μg/ml after 24 h of incubation [50]. The study found that increasing CD concentrations improved HEK-293 cell viability, indicating that the CDs themselves were biocompatible, aligning with previous literature [51]. Wang et al. found that RAW 264.7 cell proliferation increased with increased CDs concentration, possibly due to hormesis [52].

However, a concentration-dependent response indicated that higher concentrations of CDs (850 µg/ml) correlated with decreased cell viability on MCF-7. In line to many of the previous studies, we concluded that CDs have concentration and time-dependent cytotoxic potentials over MCF-7 cells. For example, Simarouba glauca-based CDs have been reported to have an LD50 of 77.02 μg/ml, demonstrating their efficacy against the MCF-7 cell line [53]. In a cellular viability experiment, carbon dots were found suppress cancer cell viability and inhibit cell migration in a time- and dose-dependent manner in 16 cell lines [54]. The results of the MTT assay indicated that the CDs of FA analog with PMX exhibited low toxicity up to a substantial concentration (1.2 mg/ml) [3]. It shown that the six types of carbon natural dots (CNDs) display low cytotoxicity toward human keratinocyte HacaT cells, MCF-7 cells, and HT-29 colon adenocarcinoma cells when treated at 500 μg/ml. Moreover, confocal microscopic cell imaging revealed that the fluorescent CND-Bamboo I particles were located on the MCF-7 cell membrane and inside the cells after treatment for 6 and 24 h, respectively [55]. It demonstrated that mesenchymal stem cell viability decreased to 35 % when incubated with 1 mg/ml CQDs [56]. High concentrations of carbon dots (CDs) from N. oleander extract show cytotoxicity on MCF-7 cells, causing severe DNA damage and cell cycle arrest, indicating the potential to induce apoptosis in breast cancer cells. These CDs, with their tiny size and oxygen content, target tumor cells selectively [57]. It proved that the features of CDs, particularly surface charge, concentration, and exposure period, affect how toxic they are.

As is the case with any medication or nanotherapeutic, the degree of toxicity usually relies on the dosage. Due to the various potential variations in the physiochemical properties of carbon dots, the likelihood of uncommon adverse interactions is also possible [16]. Typically, carbon dots exhibit minimal toxicity below a concentration of 200 µg/ml when considering a threshold of 80 % cell viability over a period of 24 h [16,58,59]. This determination is based on the majority of reported studies that have utilized data from single administration dosages [16]. Nerium oleander -based CDs have shown concentration and time-dependent cytotoxic potentials over MCF-7 cells, and could cause severe DNA damage, forming COMET tail and micronuclei. They interfere with cell-cycle progression, leading to cell arrest in G0/G1 phases and potentially triggering MCF-7 cell apoptosis. The structure and surface properties of CDs may induce oxidative stress responses and interfere with the cell cycle machinery. This work suggests that CDs with a small size and high amount of oxygen on the surface can specifically affect tumor cell function, making them potential anti-cancer therapeutic materials [57]. Further studies are needed to investigate the mutagenicity or carcinogenicity potency of CDs in mammalian cells.

To evaluate the induction of cell apoptosis by CDs, flow cytometry analysis was performed using Annexin V-FITC and PI staining. 4T1 Cells treated with CDs (850 μg/ml) don’t display a noticeable in apoptotic populations compared to the control group (Fig. 6B). The percentage of late apoptotic cells exhibited a concentration-dependent rise, indicating that CDs triggered the apoptosis in a dose-dependent manner in MCF-7 cells (Fig. 6C). The proportions of early apoptotic cells in the control and CDs groups were determined to be 0.012 % and 24.5 %, respectively. Consequently, the CDs (850 μg/ml) group exhibited the population of apoptotic cells over the duration of 24 h. It can be concluded that CDs had the ability to induce a greater amount of MCF-7 cell apoptosis compared to control groups.

The findings of our study indicate minimal cytotoxic effects of CDs following a 24-hour exposure to cellular entities. However, it is noteworthy that only the CDs characterized by a high quantum yield exhibited a considerable level of toxicity towards the cells, particularly at the peak concentrations of 2.0 and 1.5 mg/ml, in comparison to the remaining CDs at comparable concentrations [60]. These findings suggest that carbon dots could be an alternative and complementary medicine for the treatment of cancers. The interference of CDs with cell-cycle progression resulted in cell arrest in G0/G1 phases which showed that they can interact with genetic material and could trigger MCF-7 cell apoptosis [57].

The research synthesized Pemetrexed-based self-targeted carbon dots, which have dual fluorescence imaging and therapeutic action capabilities. The CDs were synthesized using a straightforward and uncomplicated single-step procedure. It is worth noting that the CDs exhibit low toxicity up to a substantial concentration that can be suitable in cell imaging experiments. Our findings indicate that CDs exhibit concentration and time-dependent cytotoxic effects on MCF7 cells, but paradoxically, promote the proliferation of healthy 4T1 cells, even at the highest CD concentrations. The interference of CDs with cell cycle showed that they material and could trigger MCF-7 cell apoptosis. The aforementioned phenomenon may arise as a result of the existence of the residuals of Pemetrexed that specifically interact with FR, thereby inducing the penetration of substantial quantities of CDs into the cellular environment. It highlights their potential as innovative cancer therapy tools. The inherent targetability of CDs can enhance their scope of utilization. For example, they can be designed for the delivery of anticancer drugs to combat cancer. Generally, these nanoparticles provide a range of distinct benefits: (1) the high level of brightness, strong stability, the presence of FA residues on the surface of the CDs enables specific recognition and targeting of FR, thereby facilitating the easy identification of cancer cells expressing FR. These highly luminous CDs have significant potential for widespread use in various biological research and biomedical applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research protocol was approved and Supported by the Student Research Committee, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences (grant number: 71619).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Paganin-Gioanni, E. Bellard, L. Paquereau, V. Ecochard, M. Golzio, J. Teissié, Radiology and oncology, 44 (2010), p. 142.
